sections.heading: KEY REGULATORY INSTRUMENTS FOR THE ACTUALIZATION OF CLOUD
sections.title: Cloud computing policy framework for public sector adoption
sections.summary: This section establishes the policy framework for cloud computing adoption by Nigerian public institutions and SMEs. It defines key terms including Cloud Computing, Cloud First Policy, Government Data, and Federal Open Data Initiative. The policy aims to promote cloud adoption as an efficient way to acquire and deploy computing resources while reducing capital costs and improving service delivery. The framework specifies that cloud computing is a model for ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to shared computing resources.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides foundational definitions and policy context for cloud computing adoption, relevant to DTIAs as it establishes the regulatory environment for cloud services and data handling in Nigeria. It does not specify transfer mechanisms or data handling requirements.

requirements.text: Small Medium and Enterprises (SMEs): refers to enterprises which have an annual turnover not exceeding Five Hundred Thousand Naira (N500,000).
Public Institutions (PIs): means Ministries, Departments, Extra-Ministerial Departments and Agencies of Government at Federal, State and Area Council levels.
Federal Public Institutions (FPIs): means Ministries, Departments, Extra-Ministerial Departments and Agencies of Government at the Federal level.

Cloud Computing: refers to computing model for ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand and real time network access pool of configurable and rapidly provisioned computing resources (networks, servers, storage, applications and services among others) required by and available to FPIs and SMEs to carry out their businesses and operations.

Cloud First Policy: refers to the Federal Government of Nigeria's strong commitment and support for cloud computing service adoption, especially from a local cloud service providers, as a first choice consideration while deploying and accessing computing resources in the public sector and by the SMEs that provide computing services to the public sector.
Government Data: Data produced or commissioned by government or government controlled entities.

Federal Open Data Initiative: refers to an initiative of the Federal Government through the Federal Ministry of Communications (FMC) aimed at ensuring substantial amount of public and non-sensitive data is released and used through digital platforms for the creation of innovative digital services that promote the country's social-economic development.
Cloud Migration: refers to the process of moving data, applications, hardware, software, network infrastructure and/or other business elements and services to a cloud computing environment.

Cloud Adoption: refers to the process or strategy that provides incentives for the public institutions and SMEs to use the cloud computing for their computing requirements in way that is efficient and sustainable.
Artificial Intelligence (AI): connotes the creation of intelligent objects that work and react like humans to carry out certain tasks meant for intelligent beings without human intervention.

Machine Learning (ML): connotes algorithm and programming that provides AI the ability to detect patterns in the data presented by smart systems, so as to learn from these patterns and improve actions from experience without human intervention

Internet of Things (IoTs): refers to the combination of concepts and technologies for embedding computer systems and the internet into all the artifacts/physical things in the real world in order to automatically collect, communicate and exchange data with one another through digital platforms.

Virtualization: refers to the concepts and technologies that allow creation of a virtual version of a device or resource, such as server, storage device, network or operating system through a framework that divides the resource into one or more execution environments
Encryption: refers to a process of converting data or information into a code to prevent unauthorised access by human and/or computer systems.
On-premise: refers to computer systems that are located within the physical confines of Federal Public Institutions and SMEs in Nigeria.
Cloud Service Providers (CSPs): refer to local and/or international cloud computing service providers rendering service to FPIs and SMEs in Nigeria.
Cloud Consulting Service Providers (CCSP): refer to local cloud computing company or individual professional proving cloud computing consulting services to FPIs and SMEs in Nigeria.
Virtual Machine: refers to a software program or operating system that exhibits the behaviour of a separate computer and capable of performing tasks such as running applications and programs.
Vendor lock-in: refers to a situation in which FPI or SME using the cloud product or service of a cloud service provider cannot easily transition to competitor's cloud product or service.

sections.source_quote: KEY REGULATORY INSTRUMENTS FOR THE ACTUALIZATION OF CLOUD 23.0

sections.title: Cloud adoption policy for public sector and SMEs
sections.summary: This section outlines the policy framework for cloud adoption by Nigerian public institutions and SMEs, emphasizing the 'Cloud First' approach. It specifies that the policy applies to Federal Public Institutions, State and Local Government entities, and government-owned corporations. The policy aims to achieve a 30% increase in cloud adoption by 2024 among public sector entities and SMEs providing digital services to the government, while also targeting a 35% growth in cloud computing investments by 2024. The policy mandates that public-sector entities prioritize cloud-based procurement whenever possible to reduce costs and improve service delivery.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes the policy requirements for cloud adoption in Nigeria, relevant to DTIAs as it mandates cloud-first procurement for public sector entities and specifies data handling requirements for government data. It does not address cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: The Nigeria Cloud Computing Policy is issued pursuant to Section 6 (a) (b) (c) and (i) of the National Information Technology Development Act 2007.

The Policy is applicable to all Federal Public Institutions, Public Institutions at the State and Local Government levels. The Policy shall also apply to all corporations fully or partially owned by the Federal Government in Nigeria, as data generated by these institutions constitute data that is regarded as "Government Data".
Upon the publication of this policy, Nigerian public-sector entities shall prioritise the procurement of cloud-based information and communication technologies (ICTs), whenever possible.

The goal of this Policy is to ensure a 30% increase in adoption of cloud computing by 2024 among Federal Public Institutions (FPIs) and SMEs that provide digital-enabled services to the government. The policy also targets 35% growth in cloud computing investments by 2024.
The policy also aims to create an enabling environment for more investment in Cloud infrastructure and platforms.
This policy applies regardless of whether the ICT solution under procurement is destined for end users in government service, for citizen use, or for government data centre needs.

sections.source_quote: The Nigeria Cloud Computing Policy is issued pursuant to Section 6 (a) (b) (c) and (i) of the National Information Technology Development Act 2007.

sections.heading: (continued)
sections.title: Cloud Computing Service Models and Deployment Options
sections.summary: This section describes cloud computing service models (SaaS, PaaS, IaaS) and deployment models (private, public, hybrid clouds). It outlines how public institutions in Nigeria can leverage these models for efficient service delivery, cost savings, and resource optimization without managing underlying infrastructure. The section emphasizes transitioning from traditional IT systems to cloud solutions to improve responsiveness, transparency, and resource allocation while avoiding vendor lock-in.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines cloud service models and deployment options critical for DTIA as it specifies how data storage and processing must align with Nigerian territorial boundaries and security requirements for different data sensitivity levels. It mandates that public institutions assess cloud solutions based on data classification to ensure compliance with national security and data protection standards.

requirements.text: Public Institutions will leverage cloud services to provide improved responsiveness to citizens' needs and increased transparency in healthcare, social amenities, justice, public safety, and education services.
Cloud technologies will create competitive advantages for SMEs by generating employment opportunities, developing indigenous technology, and diversifying the economy.
Migrating to cloud can streamline processes by consolidating dispersed IT systems under a central government platform, reducing costs through 'as needed' resource procurement.
Public institutions must use cloud services to move process-oriented tasks like server maintenance to providers, allowing IT professionals to focus on value-adding skills.
Cloud service providers must offer infrastructure that allows provisioning of processing, storage, networks, and other computing resources without managing underlying infrastructure.
Cloud service providers must provide security certifications assessed by third-party professionals to ensure data security.

sections.source_quote: Cloud computing allows for new features to be continuously deployed, while the costs are amortised across a global service customer base.

sections.heading: (continued)
sections.title: Cross-Border Data Flows and Data Classification for Cloud Services
sections.summary: This section addresses cross-border data flows in cloud environments, requiring public institutions to ensure cloud service providers store data in jurisdictions with equivalent data protection standards to Nigeria. It mandates data classification based on sensitivity levels (official, confidential, secret, classified) to determine appropriate cloud storage locations and security measures, with classified data restricted to Nigerian territory or private cloud solutions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is critical for DTIA as it specifies jurisdictional requirements for cross-border data flows and mandates data classification to ensure compliance with Nigerian territorial boundaries and security standards for different data sensitivity levels.

requirements.text: Public Institutions must contract cloud service providers that store data in jurisdictions with equivalent personal data protection standards to Nigeria.
Data classified as 'official, public or non-confidential' may be moved to cloud services without restrictions.
Confidential data must reside primarily within Nigerian territorial boundaries but can be accessed internationally for economic purposes.
Secret data must reside primarily within Nigerian territorial boundaries with encryption and strong authentication.
Classified data must remain within Nigerian territory or private cloud solutions, with no international access.

sections.source_quote: Agencies shall consider focusing on access to data when required. To this end, NITDA will ensure that CSPs provide adequate security and privacy measures and transparency around data compliance.

sections.heading: (continued)
sections.title: Cloud service contracts for government entities
sections.summary: This section mandates that NITDA develops a regulatory framework for cloud computing contracts between public institutions and cloud service providers (CSPs). The framework ensures government entities receive rights equivalent to individual customers, including minimum contractual requirements such as CSP due diligence compliance, service descriptions, contract duration, payment terms, SLAs, data handling rules, data retrieval rights upon termination, liability limitations, and security certifications. These provisions establish baseline contractual obligations for secure and transparent cloud service relationships.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires contractual safeguards for data handling, termination rights, and liability limitations when transferring data to CSPs, directly impacting cross-border data transfers if government entities use international CSPs.

requirements.text: NITDA will develop a regulatory framework for cloud computing contracts between Public Institutions and Cloud Service Providers (CSPs)
Government entities using cloud as customers must enjoy at least the same rights as individual customers, enterprises and other cloud customers
Contracts must include CSP adherence to due diligence process and public procurement guidelines
Contracts must specify service descriptions
Contracts must define duration unless unlimited
Contracts must include payment terms and termination clauses
Contracts must detail Service Level Agreements (SLAs)
Contracts must specify rules for handling cloud customer data including processing, destruction and restoration
Contracts must include CSP customer care services depending on service offering
Contracts must provide customers' right to retrieve data upon termination
Contracts must limit CSPs' right to exclude liability unreasonably or impose unfair terms related to data loss, damage, service degradation or breaches
Contracts must specify level of cloud security and privacy

sections.source_quote: NITDA will work on the development of a regulatory framework for the execution of cloud computing contracts between Public Institutions and Cloud Service Providers (CSP). This regulatory framework shall ensure that government entities using the cloud as cloud customers enjoy at least the same rights as those enjoyed by individual customers, enterprises and other cloud customers.
